coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of broad-spectrum tunable photoluminescent organosilicon nanodots from lac dye for cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Lanxiang
Li, Xiang
Li, Kun
Feng, Changmeng
Gao, Zixue
Ma, Jinju
Xu, Juan
Zhang, Hong
coredata.dc:description: To further develop the fluorescence detecting application of lac dye, a novel organosilicon nanodots LSiNDs was synthesized via a simple one-step hydrothermal reaction between lac dye and organic silane. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy demonstrated that the LSiNDs are rich in abundant oxygen-containing functional groups (silicon-oxygen, hydroxyl, and amide groups) and have a polyaromatic structure. The LSiNDs demonstrated a broad-spectrum tunable photoluminescent performance with a narrow size distribution (2.6 ± 0.9 nm). They can emit blue, green, orange, and red fluorescence from 420 nm to 900 nm by varying the excitation wavelengths. In particular, it can emit fluorescence between 770 nm and 900 nm in the near infrared region, which has the advantages of strong tissue penetration and little autofluorescence interference. In addition to their excellent stability and biocompatibility, LSiNDs have a fluorescence quantum yield of up to 49% at 350 nm excitation. Finally, the LSiNDs have been effectively applied as a sensitive fluorescent probe for HepG2 live cell imaging. So, this work expands on how lac dye can be used in fluorescence imaging and other fields of biology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lac dye
Organosilicon nanodots
Tunable photoluminescent
Broad-spectrum
Near infrared
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization and composition of the LSiNDs
Fluorescence lifetime and FLQYs of the LSiNDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The illustration of the green route of GQDs synthesis. (B) Proposed mechanism for the synthesis of the LSiNDs.

(A) TEM image of LSiNDs, where the inset shows the size distribution. (B) HR-TEM image of the LSiNDs (C) XRD pattern of the LSiNDs. (D) FTIR spectrum of the LSiNDs. XPS spectra of the LSiNDs: the XPS survey spectrum (E) and the high-resolution spectra of C 1s (F), N 1s (G), O 1s (H) and Si 2p (I).

(A) Photographs of LSiNDs solutions under white light and UV light (365 nm) irradiation, respectively. 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 and 6 represent LSiNDs solutions with concentrations of 3.0, 2.0, 1.2, 1.0, 0.75, and 0.3 mg mL−1, and 7 represents 0.1 mg mL−1 lac dye, respectively. (B) UV–vis absorption of LSiND solution (1.2 mg mL−1). (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of LSiNDs solutions with concentrations of 3.0, 2.0, 1.5, 1.2, 1.0, 0.75, 0.6, 0.5, 0.4 and 0.3 mg mL−1 at the excitation of 360 nm. (D) Fluorescence emission spectra of 1.2 mg mL−1 LSiNDs solution at the excitation of 280 nm. (E) Corresponding PL spectra at different excitation wavelengths from 300 nm to 400 nm. (F) PL excitation and NIR emission spectra of the LSiNDs.

(A–C) 3D fluorescence spectra of the 1.2 mg mL−1 LSiNDs. (D) Excitation spectrum (purple line) and emission spectrum in NIR region (red) of the LSiNDs. (E) Excitation spectrum (cyan) and emission spectrum in green region (green) of the LSiNDs. (F) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of the LSiNDs solution at emission wavelength of 445, 560 and 840 nm.

FL intensities of the LSiNDs in (A) different pH, (B) different concentrations of hydrogen peroxide solutionand (C) different ionic strength, (D) different solutions of metal ions (1.0 mmol L−1), (E) sodium salts containing different anions (1.0 mmol L−1), and (F) different temperatures.
Cell viability assay of the LSiNDs at different concentrations.

Confocal microscopy images of the LSiNDs at different incubation time points in HepG2 cells. The cells were incubated with 1.2 mg mL−1 LSiNDs for 2, 16, and 24 h, respectively. Before imaging, the cells were stained with Hoechst 33,342 for 10 min to visualize nuclei. Confocal microscopy imaging of the cells with different imaging channels: (A) bright-field image. (B) Excitation channel: 461 nm. (C) Excitation channel: 495 nm. (D) Excitation channel: 359 nm. (E) Merged channel. All bar present 30 μm.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of broad-spectrum tunable photoluminescent organosilicon nanodots from lac dye for cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Highland Forest Science, Chinese Academy of Forestry, Research Center of Engineering and Technology of Characteristic Forest Resources, National Forestry and Grassland Administration, Kunming, 650233, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Southwest Forestry University, Kunming, 650224, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Science, Southwest Forestry University, Kunming, 650224, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Xinyang Agriculture and Forestry University, Xinyang, 464000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Xinyang Agriculture and Forestry University, Xinyang 464000, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Institute of Highland Forest Science, Chinese Academy of Forestry, Research Center of Engineering and Technology of Characteristic Forest Resources, National Forestry and Grassland Administration, Kunming, 650233, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Center of Engineering and Technology of Characteristic Forest Resources

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To further develop the fluorescence detecting application of lac dye, a novel organosilicon nanodots LSiNDs was synthesized via a simple one-step hydrothermal reaction between lac dye and organic silane. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy demonstrated that the LSiNDs are rich in abundant oxygen-containing functional groups (silicon-oxygen, hydroxyl, and amide groups) and have a polyaromatic structure. The LSiNDs demonstrated a broad-spectrum tunable photoluminescent performance with a narrow size distribution (2.6 ± 0.9 nm). They can emit blue, green, orange, and red fluorescence from 420 nm to 900 nm by varying the excitation wavelengths. In particular, it can emit fluorescence between 770 nm and 900 nm in the near infrared region, which has the advantages of strong tissue penetration and little autofluorescence interference. In addition to their excellent stability and biocompatibility, LSiNDs have a fluorescence quantum yield of up to 49% at 350 nm excitation. Finally, the LSiNDs have been effectively applied as a sensitive fluorescent probe for HepG2 live cell imaging. So, this work expands on how lac dye can be used in fluorescence imaging and other fields of biology.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Lac dye is a natural red dye that originates from the secretion of the Kerria Lacca insect. It has been widely used in food [1], cosmetics [2], textiles [3,4] and works of art [5] for a long time. Lac dye is a mixture, including laccaic acid A, B, C, D, and E (Fig. 1 A), among which laccaic acid A accounts for about 85% of the total mass [4,6]. These five kinds of laccaic acids are all anthraquinone compounds, which have better planar and conjugated structures. Thus, lac dye can emit orange (∼580 nm) and red (∼660 nm) fluorescence [7,8] in different solvents and pH solutions, with wide an emission spectrum and full utilization of spectral energy. This colorful fluorescence performance of lac dye endows the possibility of applications in food detection, light-emitting diodes, biological imaging, and so on. However, the value of lac dye in fluorescence detection has been significantly underestimated [9], and its applications are usually limited due to drawbacks such as low photoluminescent (PL) quantum efficiency, specific excitation wavelengths, and susceptibility to oxidative bleaching. The polyaromatic structure (anthraquinone) of laccaic acid is the key framework for its fluorescence emission [10,11]. At the same time, its molecule contains multiple hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, which provide active sites for its chemical modification and preparation of nanomaterials [12,13]. Benefit its advantages of chemical structure, our previous work has shown that a synthesis carbon quantum dot from lac dye is an effective way to expand its application in fluorescence detection [14]. Thus, we expect to further develop lac dye into novel nanomaterials with better fluorescence performance for fluorescence imaging.

Fluorescent probes play a crucial role in fluorescence imaging, which is one of the most widely used methods for non-invasive real-time sensing of living systems and disease diagnosis due to its obvious advantages, which include good spatial and temporal resolution, high sensitivity, selective minimum invasiveness, and excellent tolerate limit [15–17]. The ideal fluorescent probe should possess excellent photostability, high fluorescence quantum yield, high sensitivity, and selectivity [18,19]. Specifically, fluorescent probes should be able to penetrate biological tissues without causing tissue damage. In addition, the spontaneous blue fluorescence of biological tissues causes significant fluorescence background that interferes with the extraction and analysis of fluorescent probe signals [20]. Therefore, fluorescent probes with biosafety, high sensitivity and tunable PL property are being widely developed [21,22]. At present, many fluorescent probes, including fluorescein [23], inorganic fluorescents [24], nanomaterials such as carbon quantum dots [25–27], supramolecular [11] and organosilicon nanodots [28,29], and molecular beacons [30] have been developed and applied in fluorescence imaging. Among these, functionalized organosilicon nanodots have received great attention because of their excellent photostability, high fluorescence quantum yield, high sensitivity, and selectivity [29,31].

Functionalized organosilicon nanodots are a type of typically PL nanomaterials [28,32]. Their good optical properties, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and environmental protection are a result of their programmability of physical and chemical characteristics, presence of reactive functional groups, a large surface to volume ratio, and optimal size [29,31]. Many studies have shown that the introduction of silicon atoms into some organic dye molecules in order to prepare functionalized organosilicon nanodots can promote spectral redshift and improve the photo stability of dye molecules [32,33], which are suitable for use as fluorescent probes with excellent photo physicochemical properties [34,35].

In this work, a simple one-step hydrothermal reaction between lac dye and (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES) is used to produce novel functionalized organosilicon nanodots (named LSiNDs). The LSiNDs have broad-spectrum tunable PL. In addition to the fluorescence emission in blue, green, and orange regions that have a high fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY), the LSiNDs also emit red and NIR fluorescence. Moreover, the LSiNDs have excellent stability and biocompatibility, which have been successfully applied to HepG2 live cell imaging.

Lac dye was purchased from Mojiang Senyuan Technology Co., Ltd (Puer, Yunnan, China). (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES), rhodamine B and ethanol were bought from Aladdin Industrial Inc. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Deionized water was used in the experiments.

30.0 mg of lac dye was weighed and added to 20 mL of water and ultrasonically treated for 15 min. The mixture was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and 1.0 mL APTES was added, which was then sealed and maintained at a temperature of 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resultant solution was filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane filter. The filtrate (30.0 mg mL−1) was diluted 10 to 100 times to obtain LSiNDs solutions with different concentrations, and the solid powder LSiNDs were obtained from the filtrate after vacuum distillation.

Experiments using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were conducted on a FEI talos F200x G2 operating at 200 kV. The Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90 was used to measure the sizes of the LSiNDs particles. X-ray diffraction (XRD) tests were carried out on an UltimaIV X-ray, Cu Ka radiation diffractometer, voltage 40 kV, current 40 mA. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were collected using the Tenson 27 device (Brook, MI, USA), with a scanning range set from 4000 to 400 cm−1 and a resolution ratio of 4 cm−1. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with a 400 μm beam spot and a voltage of 12 kV. The mass spectrum (ESI-MS) was recorded on an Agilent G6230 TOF (MALDI-TOF MS). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectra were obtained by an Agilent Cary Series UV–Vis spectrometer. Fluorescence spectra were collected using a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer with an excitation wavelength range of 280–520 nm and a pitch of 10 nm. The scan rate was 2400 nm/min and the photomultiplier tube voltage (PTV) was 850 V. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured on the Edinburgh Spector fluorimeter FS5. The source was a 375 nm EPL laser with a 1.0 nm increment and a 0.01 nm monochromator bandwidth.

The standard reference for the FLQY was 1,4-Bis(5-phenyl-2-oxazolyl)benzene (POPOP, dissolved in cyclohexane, FLQY = 97%), quinine sulphate (dissolved in 0.1 mol L−1 H2SO4, FLQY = 58%), and Rhodamine B in water (FLQY = 31%) [28], which was calculated using the following equation
Φ =
Φ R
×
S
S R
×
A R
A
×
(
η
η R
)
2
where Φ is FLQY, S is the integrated area of the emission spectrum, A is the UV–vis absorbance at the fluorescence excitation wavelength, and η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript R stands for standard reference.

The cytotoxicity of LSiNDs was evaluated using the CCK-8 assay. HepG2 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% Penicillin-Streptomycin in an incubator at 37 °C and 5% CO2. Cells in cell medium were seeded in 96-well plates for 24 h and then incubated with different concentrations of LSiNDs (0, 0.075, 0.15, 0.30, 0.60, 0.90, 1.2, 1.2, and 1.5 mg mL−1) in cell culture medium at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h. Afterwards, 10 μL of CCK-8 reagent was added to each well and the cells were cultured in an incubator at 37 °C for 4 h. The absorbance at 450 nm was measured by an enzyme standard instrument. The inhibition of cell growth was calculated by the following formula:
C e l l
v i a b i l i t y
( % )
=
A n
A 0
× 100 %
where An is the absorbance value of the treated group, and A
0 is the absorbance value of the untreated group.

HepG2 cells in the logarithmic growth phase were digested and counted, and the concentration was adjusted to 1 × 104 mL−1. 500 μL of cell fluid per well was seeded in a 24-well plate, which was subsequently incubated with 1.5 mg mL−1 LSiNDs in cell culture medium at 37 °C for 2, 16, and 24 h, respectively. After removing the cells, the nuclei were stained with DAPI for 15min, washed three times with PBS, and observed under a confocal microscope (Ex: 359 nm, Em: 461 nm; Ex: 495 nm, Em: 519 nm; Ex: 557 nm, Em: 572 nm) fluorescence distribution, and images were collected.

As shown in Fig. 2 A, the fluorescent LSiNDs were synthesized via a simple one-step hydrothermal reaction between lac dye and APTES. Due to the harsh preparation conditions and carbonization reaction, the formation of LSiNDs is a difficult process. Combined with previous studies [28,32,36], Fig. 2B shows the proposed mechanism for the production of the LSiNDs. Initially, APTES was hydrolyzed in water and polymerized to form corresponding silanol condensates. The lac dye was then carbonized at high temperature and pressure to form small carbon dots (LCDs) with a large number of hydroxyl groups on the surface. Finally, the silicon-hydroxyl group and the hydroxyl group on the surface of the carbon point undergo further dehydration reactions and chemical bonding to produce the LSiNDs. The mass spectrum shows that the molecular weight of LSiNDs may be as high as 2643 (Fig. S1), which is clearly much larger than the molecular weight of lac dye or APTES, indicating that the polymerization reaction occurs in the process of synthesis of the LSiNDs [30,32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and composition of the LSiNDs
ce:section.ce:para: TEM was used to observe the morphology and size distribution of the LSiNDs. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the LSiNDs had torispherical nanodots, their particle size distribution was uniform and non-agglomerated, and their average particle size was (2.6 ± 0.9) nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) analysis indicated that a small number of dots had well-defined lattice fringes, with an average in-plane lattice spacing of 0.25 nm (Fig. 3B). This may be due to the presence of amorphous carbon [34,37]. The X-ray diffractometry (XRD) pattern of the LSiNDs (Fig. 3C) reveals a broad peak centered at 21.5 Å (0.34 nm), indicating the amorphous nature of LSiNDs [38].

FTIR spectroscopy and XPS spectroscopy experiments were conducted to explore the chemical composition and functional groups of LSiNDs. The FTIR spectrum is shown in Fig. 3D. The absorption peaks at 3349 and 3287 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H or N–H, the weak absorption peaks at 2923 and 2866 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibration of C–H, and the absorption peaks at 1649 cm−1 indicate the presence of amide (N–CO). The absorption peaks at 1563 and 925 cm−1 correspond to the vibration of the benzene ring; the absorption peak of 1481 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the C–N bond; and the absorption peak at 1328 cm−1 reveals the presence of the C–O bond. In the meantime, the stretching vibrations of Si–O–Si and Si–O–H bonds are observed at 1127 and 1013 cm−1, respectively.

Fig. 3E shows the XPS survey spectrum of the LSiNDs, which indicates that the LSiNDs are primarily composed of C (50.4%), N (11.1%), O (26.7%) and Si (11.8%) elements. As shown in Fig. 3F, three types of peaks at 288.61, 286.19, and 284.64 eV are found in the C 1s high-resolution spectrum, which correspond to the CO, C–N or C–O, and C–C or C–H bonds, respectively. The presence of C–N–C or N–H3+ (R = C/H) at 401.25 eV and C–NH2 at 399.29 eV was revealed by the N 1s high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 3G). O1s can be divided into 533. 5 and 532.18 two peaks of the eV, corresponding to C–O and CO or Si–O (Fig. 3H). The presence of these silicon-containing bonds can also be verified by the Si 2p signal at 102.49 eV, which was attributed to the Si–O (Fig. 3I). The LSiNDs surface components identified by XPS were consistent with the results of FTIR, indicating that the surface of the synthesized LSiNDs contains silicon-oxygen bond, hydroxyl, amide, and amino groups [28,37,39].

The optical properties of LSiNDs are demonstrated in Fig. 4 . The synthesized LSiNDs are well dispersed in water, the solution is clear and uniform, and the solution color ranges from brown to pale yellow as the concentration decreases at daylight, while lac dye solution is red (Fig. 4A). Fig. 4B shows the UV–vis spectrum, the strong absorption peak in the region of about 216 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition, and the peak at 274 nm is attributed to the n-π* transition. Additionally, there is a wide characteristic band range from 392 to 500 nm, which is the π-π*transition of the polyaromatic structure [37]. Under 365 nm UV light irradiation, LSiNDs emitted strong orange to cyan fluorescence as concentration decreased, but the red fluorescence emitted by the raw lac dye is weaker (Fig. 4A). As shown in Fig. 4C, at the excitation of 360 nm, the fluorescence peak of LSiNDs between 375 and 640 nm is wide and strong, and the range of the fluorescence peak does not change with the change of its concentration. However the fluorescence intensity increases first and then decreases, indicating that the appropriate aggregate particle size of LSiNDs is conducive to the emission of stronger fluorescence [14,28].

The fluorescence spectra of the LSiNDs are excitation-dependent (λex-dependent) with an excitation range from 280 to 520 nm (Fig. 4D, E, and 4F), indicating that surface defects may be involved in the PL process. The three emission peaks of the LSiNDs are located at around 430, 570, and 850 nm, with an excitation of 280 nm (Fig. 4D). Notably, in addition to fluorescence emission in the blue, green and orange regions, the LSiNDs emit red and NIR fluorescence around 770–900 nm with irradiation at 280 nm (Fig. 4D), indicating that the LSiNDs have the advantages of strong tissue penetration and little autofluorescence interference [40,41].

Furthermore, Fig. 4E shows that among all the excitation wavelengths examined, LSiNDs have the greatest fluorescence emission at the excitation of 300 nm, and its fluorescence emission is broad and continuous from 420 to 900 nm, with two peaks at about 533 nm and 832 nm. Furthermore, the green fluorescence emitted by LSiNDs is stronger than that in the near infrared region. However, the fluorescence intensity of LSiNDs gradually decreases when the excitation wavelength is increased from 300 nm to 400 nm. When the wavelength of the excitation light went from 420 nm to 520 nm, the fluorescence emission became a single and broad peak, and the peak shifted from 724 nm to 569 nm (Fig. 4F).

The 3D fluorescence spectrum (Fig. 5 A, B and 5C) shows more clearly that LSiNDs have multiple fluorescence emission with multi-energy states, due to the multiple independent components in this LSiNDs sample [38,42,43]. Fig. 5A shows that there are two main fluorescence emissions viewed from the horizontal axis, and the fluorescence performance of the NIR region (820–900 nm) is much stronger than that of the 400–570 nm. Fig. 5B shows that the first fluorescent emission center of the LSiNDs is located at 420–470 nm, and it emits blue light. Meanwhile, the right side of the emission center extends to 570 nm, indicating that the LSiNDs are capable of emitting green fluorescence. Fig. 5C shows that the second emission center of the LSiNDs is located in the NIR region of 845–865, and it can be excited to emit red fluorescence even when the excitation wavelength is located in the range of 280–400 nm.

It is noteworthy that the intensity ratio of 850 nm–430 nm is 2.4 according to the fluorescence spectra (Fig. 4D), whereas the Stokes shift of the NIR fluorescence spectra is as large as 554 nm with the optimal excitation wavelength of 298 nm (Fig. 5D), giving the LSiNDs significant advantages for use in cell imaging [44,45]. Moreover, the LSiNDs emitting green fluorescence (550 nm) at the optimal excitation wavelength of 444 nm (Fig. 5E), which is also conducive to cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence lifetime and FLQYs of the LSiNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence lifetime of the LSiNDs experiment was conducted to explore the luminescence origins of these peaks. As shown in Fig. 5F, the fluorescence lifetime at 445, 560, and 840 nm for the LSiNDs is 9.36, 7.26, and 2.94 ns, which is the same order of magnitude as those of other silicon-based nanomaterials, indicating that the LSiNDs silane molecules are similar to those of silicon sources due to the effect of APTES molecules [28,33]. On the other hand, the three separate fluorescence lifetimes indicated that three emissions under one-wavelength excitation are from different electronic processes. Also, the fluorescence decay of 840 nm faster than other wavelengths, which shows that the 840 nm emission come from the LSiNDs’ intrinsic state [28].

To further explore the PL features of the LSiNDs, we have measured and calculated the three FLQYs, according to the related three independent emission bands, thus, 420–490 nm, 420–575 nm, and 560–685 nm (see experimental details in Table S1). POPOP was used as the 420–490 nm, with a excite band of 300 nm, and a FLQY of 31%; quinine sulphate was used as the 420–575 nm, with a excite band of 350 nm, and a FLQY of 49%; Rhodamine B was used as the 560–685 nm, with a excite band of 514 nm, and a FLQY of 28%. Basing on above experiments, we concluded that, lac dye was modified by APTES, in a high temperature of 180 °C and an aqueous phase, which induced two advantages for the targeted LSiNDs: 1) the structure unit of Si brought significant spectral tuning effect [46,47]; 2) a higher degree of polymerization [48] than non-modificated substrate of Lac dye.

Due to the possibility of using the 430, 520, and 835 nm peaks of the LSiNDs for visual sensing, the stability was investigated. Fig. 6 A shows that the fluorescence intensity of LSiNDs doesn't change much in a medium with a pH between 3 and 11, but it drops quickly in a medium with a pH of 1 or 13, where a strongly acidic or alkaline solution. Fig. 6B and C shows that the LSiNDs exhibit excellent oxidation resistance and salt tolerance [38], as the fluorescence intensity of LSiNDs almost does not change even in a 0.1 mol L−1 hydrogen peroxide solution or 2.0 mol L−1 sodium chloride medium. Furthermore, the fluorescence property of LSiNDs was investigated with 9 different cations and 10 different anions. As shown in Fig. 6D and E, except for the reduction of fluorescence intensity with iron ion (Fe3+) and mercury ion (Hg2+), the fluorescence of LSiNDs was not significantly changed by other cations and anions. In addition, the stability of this LSiNDs has been checked on different temperatures. The result shows that LSiNDs are stable at temperatures of 25 and 37 °C and decreases slightly as the temperature continues to rise (Fig. 6F). Overall, the LSiNDs offer excellent stability, which provides the possibility of being applied in complex biological imaging environments.

Cytotoxicity is a crucial factor which needs to be mainly considered for applications in the biological field [49,50]. The cytotoxicity of the LSiNDs was determined by CCK-8 assays on HepG2 cells. As shown in Fig. 7 , the proliferation and metabolic processes of HepG2 cells were unaffected by the increase in LSiNDs concentration from 0.2 to 1.5 mg mL−1, and the cell viability remained higher than 93.5%. This shows that the LSiNDs have low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, making them good for long-term imaging of cells.

To further explore if the LSiNDs can be used for cell imaging, the LSiNDs were introduced into human HepG2 cells. Prior to the observation using a confocal microscope, the LSiNDs-treated cells were stained with DIPI for nucleus labeling. As shown in Fig. 8 , the LSiNDs could effectively enter the HepG2 cells within 2 h postinoculation. Additionally, after 16 h postinoculation, all the cells exhibit the best blue, green, and yellow emissions under different imaging excitation channels, revealing a clearer morphology than the cells in the bright field. This intense fluorescent emission continued for up to 24 h, indicating that the synthesized LSiNDs have the potential for long-term tracking of HepG2 cells, which can be well utilized for bioimaging and diagnosis of tumors [50,51].

In conclusion, we have designed and demonstrated a broad-spectrum tunable PL nanomaterial LSiNDs with a narrow size distribution (2.6 ± 0.9 nm) using a simple one-step hydrothermal reaction between lac dye and APTES. The synthesis method is facile and environmentally friendly, and it is shown that the formation process of LSiNDs is composed of first hydrolyzation and following condensation, and that its surface components are identified as silicon-oxygen bond, hydroxyl, amide, and amino groups by FT-IR and XPS. The LSiNDs showed remarkable optical features, including high PLQYs (49%) and broad-spectrum tunable PL. They can emit broad and continuous blue, green, and orange fluorescence from 420 to 900 nm at different excitations. Notably, it emits red and NIR fluorescence between 770 and 900 nm, with the excitation between 280 and 400 nm. Moreover, the LSiNDs have excellent stability in different pH media and different ion solutions, oxidation resistance, and salt tolerance. Also, the LSiNDs have low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility, which has been successfully applied to HepG2 live cell imaging. Consequently, these novel functionalized organosilicon nanodots LSiNDs have great potential for fluorescence imaging and other related biological fields.

Lanxiang Liu: Data curation, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xiang Li: Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Kun Li: Writing – review & editing. Changmeng Feng: Investigation. Zixue Gao: Investigation. Jinju Ma: Methodology. Juan Xu: Methodology. Hong Zhang: Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Non-proﬁt Research Institution of CAF (CAFYBB2020SY029). The authors express sincere gratitude to everyone who participated in this research.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2023.111090.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evaluation of coloring efficacy of lac dye in comminuted meat product
